---
title: "Ch 7 — Comparing Quantities"
subtitle: "Percentages, profit and loss, simple and compound interest — the everyday algebra of money"
lesson-id: "cbse8.comparing-quantities"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 7
ncert-chapter-title: "Comparing Quantities"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.ratio-proportion", "t1.fractions-as-parts"]
unlocks: ["cbse8.direct-inverse-proportions", "cbse10.applications-trigonometry"]
applied-domains: ["databases", "ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Do you know that $\tfrac{1}{2}$ means the same as $50\%$? If not,
   revisit
   **[Fractions as Parts](../../tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)**.
2. Are you comfortable with ratios like $3 : 4$? If not, revisit
   **[Ratio and Proportion](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)**.
3. Can you compute $25\%$ of $200$ in your head?
4. If something costs $\text{₹}80$ and you sell it for $\text{₹}100$,
   would you call that a profit or a loss?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Open your wallet or imagine one. You have $\text{₹}500$. A shop offers
"$20\%$ off" on an item priced at $\text{₹}250$.

How much do you actually pay? How much do you save? Slide below to
explore.

```{ojs}
viewof price = Inputs.range([100, 1000], {value: 250, step: 10, label: "Marked price (rupees)"})
viewof discount = Inputs.range([0, 60], {value: 20, step: 1, label: "Discount %"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const saving = price * discount / 100;
  const pay = price - saving;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>Marked price:</b> &#8377; ${price}</div>
    <div><b>Discount of ${discount}%:</b> &#8377; ${saving.toFixed(2)} off</div>
    <div><b>You pay:</b> &#8377; ${pay.toFixed(2)}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Push the discount up and down. The amount-paid and amount-saved are
*proportional* to the discount percentage. The whole chapter sits on
that simple fact.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. A discount of $20\%$ on $\text{₹}500$ — is that the same number of
   rupees as $50\%$ on $\text{₹}200$?
2. If a price increases by $10\%$ and then *decreases* by $10\%$, do
   you end up at the original price?
3. You earn $\text{₹}100$ interest in one year on $\text{₹}1000$. Next
   year, would you expect the same $\text{₹}100$ — or more, or less?
4. If a bag's marked price is $\text{₹}800$ but you bought it for
   $\text{₹}600$, what fraction of the marked price was the discount?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

Question 2 has a counter-intuitive answer: $+10\%$ then $-10\%$ does
*not* return you to the start. ($100 \to 110 \to 99$.) Percentages
compose in a multiplicative way, not an additive way. That is the seed
of compound interest, the headline idea of this chapter.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Percent of a quantity

What is $35\%$ of $80$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"$35\%$" means "$\tfrac{35}{100}$".
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{35}{100} \times 80 = \tfrac{35 \times 80}{100} = \tfrac{2800}{100}
= 28$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Express as a percent

A class has $25$ students; $9$ are absent. What percentage are absent?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute $\tfrac{9}{25}$, then multiply by $100$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{9}{25} \times 100 = \tfrac{900}{25} = 36\%$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Profit or loss

A trader buys a chair for $\text{₹}1{,}200$ and sells it for
$\text{₹}1{,}500$. What is the profit, and what is the profit *percent*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Profit $= \text{SP} - \text{CP}$. Profit percent uses CP as the base.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Profit% $= \tfrac{\text{Profit}}{\text{CP}} \times 100$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Profit $= 1500 - 1200 = \text{₹}300$.
Profit% $= \tfrac{300}{1200} \times 100 = 25\%$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Discount

The marked price (MP) of a shirt is $\text{₹}800$. After a discount of
$15\%$, what is the selling price (SP)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute the discount amount, then subtract.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Discount $= 15\%$ of $\text{₹}800 = \text{₹}120$.
SP $= 800 - 120 = \text{₹}680$.

(Equivalently: SP $= 85\%$ of MP $= 0.85 \times 800 = 680$.)
</details>

### Problem 5 — Simple interest

Find the simple interest (SI) on $\text{₹}5{,}000$ for $3$ years at
$8\%$ per annum.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\text{SI} = \tfrac{P \times R \times T}{100}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{SI} = \tfrac{5000 \times 8 \times 3}{100} = \tfrac{120000}{100} =
\text{₹}1200$.

Total amount after 3 years: $5000 + 1200 = \text{₹}6200$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Compound interest, year by year

Find the compound interest (CI) on $\text{₹}1{,}000$ for $2$ years at
$10\%$ per annum, compounded annually.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Year 1: simple interest. Year 2: interest on the *new* principal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
After year 1: amount $= 1000 + 100 = 1100$. Year 2 interest is on
$1100$, not on $1000$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- After year 1: $1000 \times 1.10 = 1100$.
- After year 2: $1100 \times 1.10 = 1210$.

CI $= 1210 - 1000 = \text{₹}210$.

(Compare with SI for the same case: $\tfrac{1000 \cdot 10 \cdot 2}{100}
= \text{₹}200$. Compound interest beats simple interest by $\text{₹}10$ —
the "interest on interest".)
</details>

You have just used the same engine — *percent of a quantity* — to
compute discount, profit, simple interest, and compound interest. Four
shop-floor problems, one idea.

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Percent.** $r\%$ means $\tfrac{r}{100}$. To find $r\%$ of a quantity
$x$: compute $\tfrac{r}{100} \cdot x$.

**Profit / loss.**
$$\text{Profit\%} = \frac{\text{SP} - \text{CP}}{\text{CP}} \times 100,
\qquad \text{Loss\%} = \frac{\text{CP} - \text{SP}}{\text{CP}} \times 100.$$

**Simple interest.** $\text{SI} = \dfrac{P \cdot R \cdot T}{100}$.

**Compound interest, annual compounding.** Amount after $n$ years:
$$A = P \left(1 + \tfrac{R}{100}\right)^n,$$
and $\text{CI} = A - P$.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Percent is per-hundred — divide and you read it right.*
> *Profit on cost; discount on price displayed.*
> *Simple grows by addition; compound grows by multiplication —*
> *Each year a new pile, each pile breeds the next.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> An article was bought for $\text{₹}4{,}000$ and sold so as to gain
> $15\%$. Find the selling price.

Competent solver's path:

1. Profit $= 15\%$ of $\text{₹}4000 = \text{₹}600$.
2. SP $= \text{CP} + \text{Profit} = 4000 + 600 = \text{₹}4600$.

(Or directly: SP $= 1.15 \times \text{CP} = 1.15 \times 4000 = \text{₹}
4600$.)

**Answer: $\text{₹}4600$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Convert $\tfrac{3}{8}$ to a percentage.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{3}{8} = \tfrac{3 \times 100}{8}\% = 37.5\%$.
</details>

**W2.** A pen costs $\text{₹}50$. After a $20\%$ discount, what is the
selling price?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Discount $= \text{₹}10$. SP $= \text{₹}40$.
</details>

**W3.** SI on $\text{₹}8000$ at $12\%$ for $2.5$ years?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{8000 \times 12 \times 2.5}{100} = \tfrac{240000}{100} = \text{₹}
2400$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A shopkeeper marks his goods at $40\%$ above cost price and
gives a discount of $10\%$. Find his profit percent.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pick a convenient CP — say $\text{₹}100$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Take CP $= \text{₹}100$. MP $= 100 + 40 = \text{₹}140$.
SP $= 140 - 10\%$ of $140 = 140 - 14 = \text{₹}126$.
Profit $= 126 - 100 = \text{₹}26$.
Profit% $= 26\%$.
</details>

**P2.** Find CI on $\text{₹}8000$ for $2$ years at $5\%$ per annum,
compounded annually.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$A = P(1 + R/100)^n$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = 8000 \times (1.05)^2 = 8000 \times 1.1025 = \text{₹}8820$.
CI $= 8820 - 8000 = \text{₹}820$.
</details>

**P3.** A scooter is bought for $\text{₹}60{,}000$ and depreciates by
$10\%$ each year. Find its value after $2$ years.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Depreciation works just like compound interest, but with a *minus*
sign.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V = 60000 \times (0.9)^2 = 60000 \times 0.81 = \text{₹}48{,}600$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** The population of a town is $1{,}21{,}000$ and grows at $10\%$
per year. What was the population two years ago?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The current population is *after* two years of growth: $P_0 \times
(1.1)^2 = 121000$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P_0 = \tfrac{121000}{(1.1)^2} = \tfrac{121000}{1.21} = 1{,}00{,}000$.

**Two years ago: 1,00,000 people.**
</details>

**S2.** Sameer borrowed $\text{₹}25{,}000$ at $12\%$ p.a. compounded
*half-yearly* for $1$ year. Find the amount he repays.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Half-yearly compounding: rate per period is $\tfrac{12}{2} = 6\%$,
and there are $2$ periods.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = 25000 \times (1.06)^2 = 25000 \times 1.1236 = \text{₹}28{,}090$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** $25\%$ of $80$ is
(a) $15$ (b) $20$ (c) $25$ (d) $30$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tfrac{25}{100} \times 80 = 20$. **Answer: (b).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** A man bought a watch for $\text{₹}1{,}500$
and sold it at a loss of $8\%$. Find the selling price.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Loss $= 8\%$ of $1500 = \text{₹}120$. SP $= 1500 - 120 = \text{₹}1380$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** A sum of money becomes $\text{₹}6655$ in
$3$ years at $10\%$ per annum, compounded annually. Find the principal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$A = P(1 + R/100)^n$. Solve for $P$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$P = \dfrac{A}{(1.1)^3}$. $(1.1)^3 = 1.331$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P = \tfrac{6655}{1.331} = \text{₹}5000$.

Check: $5000 \to 5500 \to 6050 \to 6655$. ✓
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** A trader allows a discount of $10\%$ on his
marked price and still gains $20\%$. (i) If the cost price of an
article is $\text{₹}540$, find the marked price. (ii) Find the discount
amount and the selling price.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
SP $= 1.20 \times \text{CP}$ (since gain is $20\%$).
SP $= 0.90 \times \text{MP}$ (since discount is $10\%$).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

**(i)** SP $= 1.20 \times 540 = \text{₹}648$.
SP $= 0.90 \times \text{MP} \Rightarrow \text{MP} = \tfrac{648}{0.9} =
\text{₹}720$.

**(ii)** Discount $= 10\%$ of $720 = \text{₹}72$.
SP $= 720 - 72 = \text{₹}648$. ✓
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 7.5 (interlocking
discount and gain percent) is a near-twin of the 4-mark item.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2018 Set 2, Q21 ("compound interest
half-yearly") matches *Stretch S2* exactly.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Profit% is computed on the SP."** It is on the **CP**. Always.
   Discount% is on the **MP**. Mixing the bases is the most common
   error in this chapter.
2. **"$+10\%$ then $-10\%$ returns to the start."** It does not.
   ($100 \to 110 \to 99$.) Percentage changes compose multiplicatively.
3. **"Compound interest is just $n$ times simple interest."** No.
   Compound is *bigger* than that, because each year's interest itself
   earns interest the next year.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you explain to a friend, without notes, the difference between
   simple and compound interest using only one example?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Given a word problem mixing CP, SP, MP, and discount, can you sort
   them out and pick the right base for each percent?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Algebraic Expressions and Identities](../08-algebraic-expressions/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Stretch S2* and the 4-mark board item, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** redo *Discover Problem 6* — until compound interest
  feels like *interest on interest*, the chapter is not yet yours.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Ratio and Proportion](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)** — percent is a ratio out of $100$.
- **[Fractions as Parts](../../tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)** — percent is a fraction in disguise.

### What this unlocks

- **[Direct and Inverse Proportions](../11-direct-inverse-proportions/index.qmd)** — generalises proportional reasoning beyond money.
- **[Class 9 — Statistics](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/12-statistics/index.qmd)** — pie charts, frequency percentages.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 7.1–7.4: percentages, profit
  and loss, simple and compound interest.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t1.ratio-proportion`](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)
  treats proportional reasoning at the foundation level; this chapter
  applies it to money.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Every analytics dashboard reports percentages —
year-over-year growth, click-through rate, conversion percentage. The
formulas you learnt today are the ones that engineers actually type into
SQL queries.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Model accuracy ($\tfrac{\text{correct}}{\text{total}}
\times 100$) is a percent. So is precision, recall, and F1. The whole
language of evaluating a classifier is the language of this chapter.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The compound-interest formula
$A = P (1 + r/n)^{nt}$ is the discrete cousin of $A = P e^{rt}$, the
exponential growth equation that governs population biology, finance,
and bacterial colonies.
:::
